issues.description: Says water 'crosses slowly' through the bilayer. True in principle, but the script never reconciles this with the aquaporin mention in 06. Sharp students will ask: if water can cross the bilayer, why do RBCs and kidney cells need aquaporins? Add one clause clarifying that bilayer permeability to water is real but limited, and aquaporins massively accelerate it where high flux is needed.

Lists 'ion channels for sodium and potassium' as examples of facilitated diffusion. This is technically correct but glosses over a frequent AP trap: most physiologically relevant Na+/K+ channels are GATED (voltage- or ligand-gated), not always open. Students who memorize 'channels = passive, always open' get burned on action-potential questions. Worth one sentence: channels can be gated, but when open they still move ions DOWN gradient — gating controls timing, not direction.

GLUT-1 described as a carrier that 'binds the molecule, change shape, and release it on the other side' — fine, but doesn't explicitly state it is BIDIRECTIONAL and gradient-driven. Students confuse carriers with pumps because both 'change shape.' Explicitly say: carriers in facilitated diffusion are reversible and gradient-driven; pumps couple shape change to ATP hydrolysis. That distinction is the actual AP trap.

Definition 'Osmosis is a special case — the diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane' is the exact phrasing the prompt flagged as a trap. CB-aligned definition is the net movement of water across a selectively permeable membrane from a region of HIGHER WATER POTENTIAL to LOWER WATER POTENTIAL (or, equivalently, lower to higher solute). 'Diffusion of water' invites the misconception that osmosis is just a synonym for diffusion. Reword to 'net movement of water' and at minimum mention water potential, since AP FRQs use Ψ = Ψp + Ψs.

Tonicity is presented only from the cell's reference frame ('the outside has less solute than the cell'). AP questions frequently flip the frame and describe Solution A relative to Solution B, with no cell in sight. Students taught only the cell-centered version mis-label which solution is hypertonic. Add an explicit sentence: tonicity is always a COMPARISON between two solutions; whichever has MORE non-penetrating solute is hypertonic to the other, regardless of whether a cell is involved.

Tonicity vs osmolarity is not distinguished. Tonicity depends on NON-PENETRATING solutes only (e.g., a urea solution can be iso-osmotic but hypotonic because urea crosses the membrane). This is a classic Princeton Review / AP Classroom trap. At least one sentence is warranted.

'Hypotonic means the outside has less solute than the cell, so water rushes IN' — uses 'rushes' which implies active flow. Water moves by passive diffusion / down its potential gradient. Minor wording but reinforces the misconception that osmosis is energetic.

Na/K ATPase stoichiometry is correct (3 Na out / 2 K in / 1 ATP) but the script says 'inside slightly negative' as if the pump alone causes the resting membrane potential. The dominant contributor to resting potential is K+ leak channels, not the pump's electrogenic 1-charge-per-cycle contribution. Sharp students will challenge this. Acknowledge the pump 'contributes to' or 'sets up the gradient that drives' the resting potential rather than directly creating it.

Secondary active transport described correctly, but the script implies the glucose moves 'UP its own gradient.' True in intestinal epithelium but worth one clarifier — glucose in lumen can sometimes be at lower concentration than cytosol, and that is precisely WHY cotransport exists; otherwise GLUT carriers would suffice. The current wording is ambiguous about whether secondary active transport always moves the second solute uphill (it does for the coupled solute; for the driving Na+, it's still downhill).

States 'All of this uses ATP' about bulk transport. True for endocytosis and exocytosis (vesicle formation, fusion, cytoskeletal motors). But the script earlier draws a clean line of passive=no ATP vs active=ATP — bulk transport sits in a third category in most CB framing. Worth one sentence: bulk transport is ATP-requiring but is NOT classified as 'active transport' in the strict sense of pumping individual molecules against a gradient through a membrane protein.

This section narrates 'Two confusions trip up almost every AP Bio student. On the left — the common wrong intuitions. On the right — what's actually true.' But the script contains NO ACTUAL CONTENT for what the left/right columns say. Either the visual is doing all the work (in which case students with audio-only access get nothing), or the section is empty. The outline names three traps (hypertonic, facilitated=passive, osmosis=water) but the script does not state the trap or its resolution. This is a pedagogical hole — exactly the section that should hammer the misconceptions is voice-empty.

Cholesterol described only as 'keeping the membrane fluid at body temperature.' Cholesterol is a fluidity BUFFER — it DECREASES fluidity at high temperature and INCREASES fluidity at low temperature. AP frequently asks the directional question. The current phrasing is half-right and will mislead students into thinking cholesterol always increases fluidity.

Steroid hormones grouped with O2/CO2 as 'small nonpolar.' Steroids are nonpolar but not small (cholesterol, testosterone, cortisol are ~300+ Da). They cross because they are LIPID-SOLUBLE, not because they are small. Pedantic but matters — students who learn 'small nonpolar' as the only rule miss that size and polarity are independent factors.

Says plant cell in distilled water 'is actually the healthy state for a plant cell' — correct — but doesn't mention TURGOR PRESSURE explicitly as the AP-vocabulary term, or distinguish it from the wall's resistance (Ψp). FRQs use the term turgor pressure / pressure potential.

summary: Section 14 traps are voice-empty and the osmosis definition + cell-centric tonicity framing reproduce the exact AP misconceptions the module was meant to fix.
